walkthrough.title: Check Your Player Count
walkthrough.text: Before anything else, count how many players you have. The Night Cage plays one to five, but the tile mix changes depending on the number of prisoners. Grab the matching Tile Discards reference card — one side says four prisoners, the other says five. This card tells you exactly which tiles and how many of each go into the deck. For four prisoners: 4 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, and 12 Wax Eaters. For five prisoners: 5 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, and 10 Wax Eaters.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile Discards reference card for 4 Prisoners — shows all tile types and counts

walkthrough.title: Remove Unused Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now here is where it gets important. Look at your reference card and pull out any tile types that should NOT be in the deck for your player count. For a standard first game, remove ALL advanced tiles — that means Keepers, Pit Fiends, Pathless, and Omens. Set those aside in the box. Then adjust the remaining tiles to match the counts on your reference card. Any extras go back in the box. Getting this right matters — too many or too few tiles changes the game's difficulty significantly.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile Discards reference card for 5 Prisoners — different tile counts for the larger group

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Load the Tile Tower
walkthrough.text: Take all the tiles you kept and give them a thorough shuffle face-down. Do not peek at them. Now grab the tile tower — that is the tall vertical card holder with the gorgeous candle-drip artwork on the sides. Stack all the shuffled tiles face-down into the tower. They should fit snugly in a vertical stack. This tower is the heart of the game. Every time you explore, you will draw the top tile from here. When it runs empty, the final countdown begins.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile tower loaded with the shuffled tile deck

walkthrough.title: Pick Your Prisoner
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a colored Prisoner meeple. There are five to choose from — red, blue, green, yellow, and purple. These are the hooded figures that represent you wandering through the dark. Grab whichever color speaks to you. You are a prisoner trapped in an ever-shifting maze of darkness. All you have is a candle. Choose wisely — just kidding, the color is purely cosmetic. But you will get attached to your little hooded figure.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five colored Prisoner meeples — red, blue, green, yellow, and purple

walkthrough.title: Take a Player Reference Card
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one of the double-sided Player Reference cards. Start with the Normal side facing up — that is the side with the green candle art that says YOUR TURN: MOVE ONE SPACE or STAY. The other side is the Lights Out side, which you will flip to if you ever end up surrounded by darkness. Keep this card in front of you. It is your lifeline for remembering what you can do on your turn and what Nerve lets you do.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player reference card — Normal side showing Move or Stay options and Nerve powers

walkthrough.title: Deal Start Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now pull out the Start Tiles from the deck — you already counted them when checking the reference card. Deal one Start Tile to each player. Place your Start Tile face up on the table in front of you. Spread the Start Tiles apart from each other with plenty of space between them. You begin the game separated, alone in the dark. The empty space between your tiles? That is the void. You will need to explore to fill it in and find each other.
walkthrough.image_caption: Prisoner token — dark disc showing a hooded figure with a candle

walkthrough.title: Place Prisoners on Start Tiles
walkthrough.text: Put your Prisoner meeple on your Start Tile. This is where you wake up. Alone. In the dark. Your candle illuminates the tiles immediately around you — but right now, there is nothing around you. Just void. On your first turn, when you move off your Start Tile, you will draw tiles from the tower to fill in the spaces your candle reveals. The maze is about to take shape.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Monster Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the Wax Eater miniatures near the tile tower where everyone can reach them. These are the creepy gray skeletal creatures with spines and claws. They are the monsters hunting you in the dark. You will place one on the board whenever a Wax Eater tile is revealed. Also set out the Wax Eater tokens — the dark triangular tokens with the swirling flame artwork. These track Wax Eater movement.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater miniatures — gray skeletal spine creatures that hunt prisoners

walkthrough.title: Everyone Starts at Zero Nerve
walkthrough.text: Nerve is your emergency currency. Everyone starts with zero. The maximum is two. You gain Nerve by choosing to Stay on your turn instead of moving. Nerve lets you do powerful things — move again, block a monster attack, charge through a creature, or sustain a tile during the endgame. You can track it mentally, with coins, dice, or any small tokens. Two is the cap. You will never have more than two, but spending them at the right moment is everything.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lights Out side of the reference card — shows all Nerve spending options

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player and Begin
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first — youngest player, last person to be afraid of the dark, or just pick randomly. Play goes clockwise. And that is it. Setup is done. You are alone in the dark with a flickering candle, somewhere in an impossible maze that rebuilds itself around you. The only way out is together. Find the keys. Find the gates. Find each other. Ready? Take your first step into the dark.

summary.title: Check Your Player Count
bullets: Grab the Tile Discards reference card matching your player count (4 or 5)
4 Prisoners: 4 Start, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 12 Wax Eaters
5 Prisoners: 5 Start, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 10 Wax Eaters

summary.title: Remove Unused Tiles
bullets: Remove ALL advanced tiles for first game (Keepers, Pit Fiends, Pathless, Omens)
Adjust tile counts to match your reference card
Extras go back in the box

summary.title: Shuffle and Load the Tile Tower
bullets: Shuffle all remaining tiles face-down — do not peek
Stack them into the tile tower (vertical card holder)

summary.title: Pick Your Prisoner
bullets: Each player takes 1 colored Prisoner meeple (red, blue, green, yellow, or purple)
Color is cosmetic only

summary.title: Take a Player Reference Card
bullets: Each player takes 1 double-sided reference card
Start with Normal side face up (Move or Stay)
Flip to Lights Out side when surrounded by darkness

summary.title: Deal Start Tiles
bullets: Deal 1 Start Tile to each player, place face up
Spread them apart — prisoners begin separated

summary.title: Place Prisoners on Start Tiles
bullets: Place your meeple on your Start Tile
No adjacent tiles yet — the maze builds as you explore

summary.title: Set Up the Monster Supply
bullets: Place Wax Eater miniatures and tokens within reach
Miniatures go on the board when Wax Eater tiles are revealed

summary.title: Everyone Starts at Zero Nerve
bullets: All players begin with 0 Nerve (max 2)
Track with coins, dice, or memory
Gained by Staying; spent for special actions

summary.title: Choose First Player and Begin
bullets: Pick starting player by any method
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: The Core Loop
walkthrough.text: You wake up in complete darkness. All you have is a candle — and it won't last forever. Here is what every turn looks like: you either Move one space or Stay where you are. That is it. One action. But that simple choice ripples outward in terrifying ways. When you move, your candle reveals new tiles around you. When you leave, the darkness swallows those tiles back up. The maze is alive, always shifting, and it is not on your side.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player reference card — Move One Space or Stay

walkthrough.title: Moving
walkthrough.text: To move, step your Prisoner meeple one space to any orthogonally adjacent tile — up, down, left, or right. Not diagonal. If the space you move to is empty, draw the top tile from the tower and place it there. The tile's paths must connect to where you came from. After you move, look at all four spaces around your new position. Any empty space adjacent to you gets a new tile drawn from the tower and placed face up. Your candle is illuminating the area around you.

walkthrough.title: The Darkness Returns
walkthrough.text: As you move through the maze, your flickering light reveals the path around you. But here is the thing — when you leave, the darkness swallows those tiles back up. After moving and revealing new tiles, check every tile on the board. If a tile is NOT adjacent to ANY prisoner's current position, it goes dark. Remove it from the board and place it at the bottom of the tile tower. The maze literally disappears behind you. This is why staying together matters. Two prisoners standing near each other keep more tiles illuminated.
walkthrough.image_caption: A path tile — T-Bend connecting two corridors of the maze

walkthrough.title: Staying
walkthrough.text: Instead of moving, you can choose to Stay. When you Stay, pick one tile adjacent to your position and discard it to the bottom of the tower. Then gain one Nerve. The maximum Nerve is two — you cannot go above that. Staying is your way of banking emergency resources. But it costs you a tile and a turn, so use it wisely. Important: you CANNOT Stay if you are in Lights Out status. When the darkness has closed in completely, your only option is to run.

walkthrough.title: Tile Types
walkthrough.text: The maze is made of several tile types. Straights connect two opposite sides. T-Bends connect three sides. Crossroads connect all four sides. These are your basic path tiles — they form the corridors of the maze. Key tiles look special and contain a key. Gate tiles are your escape route. Start Tiles are where each prisoner began. When you draw a tile from the tower, place it so its paths connect logically to the existing maze.
walkthrough.image_caption: Path tiles in storage — the maze waiting to be built

walkthrough.title: Finding Keys
walkthrough.text: Scattered through the tile deck are Key tiles. When you move onto a Key tile, you pick up the key — it is yours now. Every prisoner needs a key to escape. You cannot share keys or trade them. Once you have your key, your next goal is finding a Gate. Keys are the first half of your escape plan. There are just enough keys in the deck for every prisoner to get one, but they could be anywhere in the tower.

walkthrough.title: Gates and Escaping
walkthrough.text: Gate tiles are your way out. But here is the catch — Gates only work when ALL prisoners are holding a Key. Until everyone has a key, Gates are just another tile. Once the last prisoner picks up their key, the race is on. Every prisoner must move to a Gate tile. When all prisoners are on Gates with Keys, everyone escapes together. You win as a group or you die as a group. No one gets left behind.

walkthrough.title: Wax Eaters
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater tile is drawn from the tower and placed on the board, put a Wax Eater miniature on it. These skeletal, spine-covered creatures are drawn to your light. At the end of each turn, Wax Eaters move one space toward the nearest prisoner. They follow paths — they do not teleport. When a Wax Eater reaches a prisoner, it attacks. The attack discards three tiles adjacent to the attacked prisoner. The darkness literally closes in around you. This can cascade — losing tiles might leave you in Lights Out.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater standee — dark disc with a cloaked skeletal figure

walkthrough.title: Lights Out
walkthrough.text: If you ever have zero illuminated tiles adjacent to your position, you are in total darkness. Flip your reference card to the Lights Out side. When you are Lights Out, everything changes. You MUST move — you cannot Stay. You are too panicked to rest. You can still spend Nerve, but your options are restricted. Getting out of Lights Out means moving to a space where new tiles can be drawn around you, reconnecting to the maze. Lights Out is terrifying, but survivable — if you keep your head.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lights Out reference card — restricted actions in total darkness

walkthrough.title: Nerve — Your Emergency Currency
walkthrough.text: Nerve is the most precious resource in The Night Cage. You gain it by Staying (one Nerve per Stay, max two). You spend one Nerve to do any of these: Move Again — take a second move this turn. Stay while Lights Out — override the panic and rest anyway. Block — reduce a monster attack from three discards to two. Charge — move onto AND through a monster tile, taking the attack but scrambling past. Sustain — during Final Flickers, prevent a tile from being removed. You cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater tokens — triangular tokens with flame art used for tracking

walkthrough.title: Block
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater attacks you and would discard three tiles around you, you can spend one Nerve to Block. Blocking reduces the attack — you only discard two tiles instead of three. That one saved tile could be the difference between staying connected to the maze and falling into Lights Out. Block is a defensive reaction, not something you do on your turn. You use it the instant an attack hits.

walkthrough.title: Charge
walkthrough.text: Sometimes a Wax Eater is blocking the only path forward. Spending one Nerve to Charge lets you move onto a monster tile and then immediately move through it to a tile on the other side. You take the full attack — three tiles discarded around you — but you end up past the monster. It is desperate and costly, but sometimes the only way through. Think of it as putting your head down and sprinting through the nightmare.

walkthrough.title: Final Flickers
walkthrough.text: When the tile tower runs empty, the endgame begins. No more new tiles will be drawn. From now on, after each player's turn, one illuminated tile adjacent to them is removed. The maze is collapsing. You can spend one Nerve to Sustain — prevent that tile from being removed this turn. But you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn, so you cannot Stay and Sustain in the same turn. During Final Flickers, every single move counts. The path to the Gates is shrinking behind you.

walkthrough.title: Winning Together
walkthrough.text: To win, every prisoner must be holding a Key and standing on a Gate tile. When the last prisoner reaches a Gate with their Key, the cage opens and everyone escapes. You all win. There is no scoring, no ranking, no individual victory. You survive together or you perish together in the dark. The difficulty scales with player count — more prisoners means more keys to find and more coordination required.

walkthrough.title: Losing Together
walkthrough.text: You lose if any prisoner becomes permanently trapped — no legal moves, no Nerve to escape, no hope. You also lose if the tile tower empties and players cannot sustain enough tiles to reach Gates before the maze collapses entirely. When the darkness wins, it wins completely. Everyone loses. There is no partial credit in The Night Cage. The maze does not care how close you got.

summary.title: The Core Loop
bullets: Each turn: Move one space OR Stay
Moving reveals new tiles, leaving removes unlit tiles
The maze shifts constantly around you

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Dark
walkthrough.text: This is a cooperative game — all four of you are prisoners trapped in an ever-shifting maze of darkness, and you will escape together or perish together. In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first two to three turns for each player so you can see how movement, tile revealing, Nerve, and Wax Eaters work. Meet our four prisoners: Player 1 (Red) is the cautious mover, Player 2 (Blue) is the explorer, Player 3 (Green) is the communicator, and Player 4 (Yellow) is still learning the ropes. Ready? Light your candle. Take your first step.
walkthrough.image_caption: Four colored Prisoner meeples ready to enter the maze

walkthrough.title: The Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Each prisoner starts alone on their own Start Tile, spread apart on the table. There is nothing around you — just void. Your candle illuminates only the tile beneath your feet. On your first move, you will step into the darkness. New tiles will be drawn from the tower and placed around you, building the maze as you go. The tile tower is full right now — roughly 80 tiles waiting to become corridors, intersections, keys, gates, and monsters.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile tower loaded and ready — the maze waiting to take shape

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Red): First Step — Cautious Move
walkthrough.text: Player 1, you are up. You step one space to the right off your Start Tile. Since that space is empty, draw the top tile from the tower and place it. It is a T-Bend — three paths branch out from your new position. Now check all four spaces around you. Any empty space adjacent to where you are standing gets a new tile drawn from the tower. Two more tiles appear around you. Your candle flickers and the maze takes shape. But look behind you — your Start Tile is still adjacent to you, so it stays illuminated. Smart. Red is staying close to known ground.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Blue): First Step — Pushing Outward
walkthrough.text: Player 2, your turn. Blue steps forward boldly. You draw a tile — it is a Straight, a simple corridor. Two more tiles fill in around you. Blue is already scanning the new tiles. No Key yet, but the corridors are opening up. Remember: every tile you reveal is a chance to find one of the six Keys hidden in the tower. Blue is exploring with purpose — moving in a consistent direction rather than doubling back.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Green): First Step — The Communicator
walkthrough.text: Green immediately starts talking: 'I have a Crossroads and two T-Bends around me. No Keys or Gates yet. I am heading south — Red, which direction are you going?' This is exactly the right instinct. In The Night Cage, calling out what you see and coordinating your direction is one of the most powerful things you can do. Green is already building the team's shared map of the maze.

walkthrough.title: Player 4 (Yellow): First Step — Into the Void
walkthrough.text: Player 4, your turn. Yellow steps off the Start Tile and draws a T-Bend. Two more tiles appear around you. So far, so good — everyone has taken their first step and the maze is starting to form in four separate clusters. But those clusters are far apart. The void between prisoners is huge. Finding each other means exploring toward the middle, closing the gaps, and linking your illuminated zones together.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Red): Turn 2 — Staying Close
walkthrough.text: Red takes another careful step, revealing more maze. But Player 1 is thoughtful about direction — Red moves toward the center of the table, angling toward where Green and Blue are exploring. Why? Because when two prisoners are adjacent, their candles overlap. Tiles between them stay illuminated instead of being swallowed by darkness. Red knows that linking up with another prisoner doubles their survival.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Blue): Turn 2 — A Key Appears!
walkthrough.text: Blue pushes forward again, and this time the tile drawn from the tower has something special on it — it is a Key tile! Player 2 moves onto it and picks up the Key. That is one down, three to go. Blue announces to the group: 'I have a Key! One of six found.' The whole team knows the count now. But Blue also notices something — the tiles they left behind on their first turn are starting to go dark. Moving in a straight line means the maze behind you disappears. Blue needs to think about where to go next.

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Green): Turn 2 — Coordinating Movement
walkthrough.text: Green heard Red is heading toward the center. Green adjusts course to angle toward Red as well. 'I am moving northwest this turn — Red, if you keep heading south, we should link up in a couple of turns.' This kind of coordination is the heart of the game. When Red and Green connect their illuminated zones, they will have a much larger safe area to work from. Green draws tiles and the corridors grow.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake 1 — Isolation
walkthrough.text: Player 4 decides to head in the opposite direction from everyone else. Yellow reasons that more ground covered means more chances to find Keys. But here is what happens: Yellow moves away, drawing tiles to the east while everyone else is converging toward the center. The tiles behind Yellow immediately go dark — no other prisoner is nearby to keep them lit. Within two moves, Yellow is alone in a narrow corridor, surrounded by void on three sides. The maze behind them has vanished. They have fewer escape routes, fewer options, and no allies anywhere close.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tiles disappearing into darkness — isolation leaves fewer paths

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stay Close to Other Prisoners
walkthrough.text: When you are adjacent, you share illumination — tiles stay lit as long as ANY prisoner is next to them. Spreading apart means more darkness and fewer safe tiles. A solo prisoner bleeds tiles every single turn they move. Two prisoners together preserve the maze between them. Yellow should have moved toward the group, not away from it.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Red): Turn 3 — Staying for Nerve
walkthrough.text: Red is in a good position — multiple tiles around them, no immediate threats. Player 1 decides to Stay. Remember what that means: Red picks one adjacent tile and discards it to the bottom of the tower, then gains one Nerve. Red now has one Nerve banked. That is an emergency resource for later — Block, Charge, Move Again, or Sustain. Red chose wisely: Stay when you are safe and your Nerve is low.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Blue): Turn 3 — Wax Eater Encounter
walkthrough.text: Blue moves forward and draws a tile from the tower. You place it, and... it has a Wax Eater on it. A skeletal, spine-covered creature appears in the corridor ahead. Place the Wax Eater miniature on the tile. Do not panic. At the end of this turn, the Wax Eater will move one space toward the nearest prisoner — which is Blue. Blue still has a turn to react. The smart play: move away next turn, keep distance, and let the Wax Eater chase you while you explore in a different direction.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater miniature — the skeletal creature hunting you in the dark

walkthrough.title: Player 3 (Green): Turn 3 — Linking Up with Red
walkthrough.text: Green takes another step toward Red, and this time the tiles connect! Green and Red are now adjacent. Their illuminated zones merge into one larger safe area. Tiles that would have gone dark are now held in place by both prisoners' candles. The group cheers — this is how you survive. Two prisoners together are exponentially safer than two prisoners apart. Green and Red can now coordinate movement, share information about what they see, and cover more ground without losing tiles.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake 2 — Wasting Nerve
walkthrough.text: Yellow has been exploring alone and finally gained one Nerve by Staying last turn. But now Yellow is impatient — they want to find a Key faster. Player 4 spends their one Nerve on Move Again, taking a second step just to reveal more tiles. No emergency. No threat. Just impatience. Next turn, a Wax Eater tile is drawn and placed adjacent to Yellow. The creature moves toward them at the end of the turn. Yellow has zero Nerve. They cannot Block the attack. The Wax Eater reaches Yellow and discards three tiles around them. The darkness crashes in.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater tokens tracking monster movement

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Nerve Is for Emergencies
walkthrough.text: Nerve is your emergency currency. Do not spend it for convenience. Save it for when a Wax Eater attacks — Block reduces the damage from three discards to two, which can be the difference between survival and Lights Out. If Yellow had saved that one Nerve, they could have Blocked the attack and kept one more precious tile. Instead, three tiles vanished and Yellow is teetering on the edge of total darkness.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Mistake 3 — Panic During Lights Out
walkthrough.text: The Wax Eater attack was devastating. Yellow now has zero illuminated tiles adjacent to their position. Flip the reference card — Yellow is in Lights Out. When you are Lights Out, you MUST move. You cannot Stay. Yellow panics and moves further east — away from the group, deeper into unknown territory. New tiles are drawn, but Yellow is now even more isolated. The corridors they reveal are a lifeline, but they lead away from Red, Blue, and Green. Yellow is lost in the dark, alone, heading the wrong direction.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lights Out reference card — you MUST move, choose your direction carefully

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Move Toward the Group
walkthrough.text: When you are in Lights Out, you MUST move — you cannot Stay. But choose your direction carefully. Move TOWARD where you last saw other prisoners. The new tiles you reveal might reconnect you to the group. Yellow should have turned west, back toward Red and Green's linked zone. Every step away from the group makes rescue harder and burns tiles from the tower. In The Night Cage, survival means togetherness.

walkthrough.title: Player 4's Redemption
walkthrough.text: The group rallies. Green calls out: 'Yellow, we are to your west! Head back toward us!' Yellow finally turns around and starts moving west. Red and Green shift their movement to angle toward Yellow's position. After two tense turns of movement, Yellow's corridor connects to the shared illuminated zone around Red and Green. Tiles link up. Light spreads. Yellow is safe again — no longer Lights Out, no longer alone. And there, on one of the newly revealed tiles between them? A Key. Yellow picks it up. The group cheers. That is two Keys found.
walkthrough.image_caption: Prisoner reconnecting with the group — shared illumination restores safety

walkthrough.title: What We Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's recap what just happened. You saw how movement reveals tiles and how the darkness swallows them back when you leave. You saw shared illumination keep the maze alive when prisoners stay close. You saw Nerve get banked wisely by Red and wasted by Yellow. You saw a Wax Eater attack and the devastating three-tile discard. And you saw Lights Out — the terrifying moment when the darkness closes in completely. Most importantly, you saw that every mistake is recoverable when the team works together.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen the tile-reveal mechanic, shared illumination, Nerve management, Wax Eater attacks, and Lights Out recovery. Remember Player 4's three lessons: stay close to other prisoners, save Nerve for emergencies, and move toward the group when you are lost. The maze is dark and it is shifting around you — but you are not alone. You have each other's light. Keep playing.

walkthrough.title: Before You Descend
walkthrough.text:
Before you continue into the full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for survival basics and common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to tile types, Nerve powers, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. The darkness is not as scary when you have a guide.

summary.title: Welcome to the Dark
bullets: Cooperative tutorial with 4 prisoners: Red (cautious), Blue (explorer), Green (communicator), Yellow (mistake-maker)
Walk through the first 2-3 turns per player
Covers tile revealing, shared illumination, Nerve, Wax Eaters, and Lights Out

bullets: Each prisoner starts alone on a separate Start Tile

Red stays close to known ground

No doubling back — purposeful exploration

bullets: Green calls out tile positions and announces direction
Coordination is the most powerful tool in a cooperative game
Building the team's shared mental map of the maze

summary.title: Player 4 (Yellow): First Step — Into the Void
bullets: Yellow steps out and reveals tiles like everyone else
Four separate clusters of maze forming around the table
The gap between prisoners is huge — need to close it

summary.title: Player 1 (Red): Turn 2 — Staying Close
bullets: Red moves toward the center, angling toward other prisoners
Adjacent prisoners share illumination — tiles stay lit longer
Linking up doubles survival chances

summary.title: Player 2 (Blue): Turn 2 — A Key Appears!
bullets: Blue finds a Key tile and picks it up — one of six found
Announce finds to the group so everyone knows the count
Tiles behind Blue are going dark — linear exploration has a cost

summary.title: Player 3 (Green): Turn 2 — Coordinating Movement
bullets: Green angles toward Red based on communicated directions
Planning to link illuminated zones within a few turns
Verbal coordination guides movement decisions

summary.title: Player 4's Mistake 1 — Isolation
bullets: Yellow heads the opposite direction from everyone else
Tiles behind Yellow go dark — no ally nearby to keep them lit
Alone in a narrow corridor with fewer escape routes

summary.title: Lesson: Stay Close to Other Prisoners
bullets: Adjacent prisoners share illumination — tiles stay lit as long as ANY prisoner is next to them
Solo prisoners bleed tiles every turn they move
Always move toward the group, not away from it

summary.title: Player 1 (Red): Turn 3 — Staying for Nerve
bullets: Red Stays: discard 1 adjacent tile, gain 1 Nerve

bullets: In Lights Out, you MUST move — but choose direction carefully
Move TOWARD where you last saw other prisoners
New tiles you reveal might reconnect you to the group

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You survived your first turns in The Night Cage
Player 4's three lessons: stay close, save Nerve, move toward the group
Every mistake is recoverable when the team works together

summary.title: Before You Descend
bullets: Check out General Tips → survival basics for beginners
Reference the Appendix → quick-reference for tiles, Nerve, and terms
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Stay Together — Shared Illumination Is Survival
walkthrough.text: This is the most important rule in The Night Cage. When two prisoners are adjacent, their candles overlap and more tiles stay illuminated. Every tile that stays lit is another option, another escape route, another turn of breathing room. A solo prisoner bleeds tiles every turn they move — the darkness swallows everything behind them. Two prisoners together preserve the maze between them. Make converging toward each other your first priority, especially in the early game.

walkthrough.title: Nerve Is for Emergencies, Not Convenience
walkthrough.text: Nerve is capped at two, and every point is precious. The most common beginner mistake is spending Nerve on Move Again just to explore faster when there is no real urgency. Save Nerve for genuine emergencies: Blocking a Wax Eater attack that would push you into Lights Out, Charging through a monster blocking the only path to a Key, or Sustaining a critical tile during Final Flickers. When you bank Nerve by Staying, think of it as loading a single bullet — you want it for the moment it truly matters.

walkthrough.title: Communicate Tile Positions
walkthrough.text: The Night Cage is cooperative, and communication is your most powerful tool. Every turn, tell your team what tiles you see around you. Announce when you spot a Key or Gate. Warn teammates when a Wax Eater is heading their direction. A simple 'I am heading south this turn' lets your partner plan their move to keep shared tiles alive. Everyone should know where the Keys are, where the Gates are, and where the Wax Eaters are heading. No secrets in the dark.

walkthrough.title: Move Toward Keys Early
walkthrough.text: Keys are shuffled randomly into the tower, so you cannot guarantee when they appear. But you can improve your odds by exploring with purpose. Move in a consistent direction — push outward from your Start Tile in a line or arc rather than doubling back. Every new tile you reveal is a chance to find a Key. The sooner everyone has their Key, the sooner you can focus on reaching Gates. Do not wait until the tower is running low to start searching.

walkthrough.title: When Lights Out Happens, Move Toward the Group
walkthrough.text: Lights Out is terrifying but survivable. When you have zero illuminated tiles adjacent to you, you MUST move — you cannot Stay. The biggest beginner mistake is panicking and moving in a random direction. Instead, move TOWARD where you last saw other prisoners. The tiles you reveal might reconnect your corridor to the group's illuminated zone. Every step away from the group makes rescue harder and wastes tiles from the tower. When lost in the dark, head for the light.

walkthrough.title: Wax Eaters: Avoid When Possible, Block When Not
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater appears, your first instinct should be avoidance. Move away, change direction, let it chase you while you explore elsewhere. This works when you have space and the Wax Eater is not blocking your path. But when avoidance is not possible — the creature is right on top of you — spend one Nerve to Block. Blocking reduces the attack from three tile discards to two. That one saved tile can prevent a cascade into Lights Out. Do not waste Nerve on Charging through Wax Eaters unless absolutely necessary.

walkthrough.title: Don't Over-Explore — You're Draining the Tile Tower
walkthrough.text: Every time you move, you draw tiles from the tower. Every tile drawn brings you closer to Final Flickers — the endgame phase where the maze starts collapsing. New players often wander aimlessly, drawing tile after tile just to see what is out there. But each tile drawn is gone from the tower. If you over-explore early, Final Flickers comes sooner and you may not have found all the Keys yet. Be purposeful. Explore toward objectives — Keys, Gates, and other prisoners. Not just for the sake of exploring.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the beginner traps that cost games. Isolating from the group — you bleed tiles and lose escape routes. Wasting Nerve on convenience — you have nothing left when a real crisis hits. Panic-moving during Lights Out — moving away from the group instead of toward it. Ignoring the tile tower count — failing to notice Final Flickers is approaching. Forgetting to communicate — your teammates cannot help you if they do not know where you are. Staying near a Wax Eater — you are giving it a free turn to close in. Avoid these and you will survive far longer.

summary.title: Stay Together — Shared Illumination Is Survival
bullets: Adjacent prisoners share illumination — more tiles survive
Solo prisoners bleed tiles every turn they move
Converge toward each other as first priority

summary.title: Nerve Is for Emergencies, Not Convenience
bullets: Max 2 Nerve — save for Block, Charge, Sustain
Don't spend Nerve on Move Again just to explore faster
Stay to bank Nerve when safe, spend when crisis hits

summary.title: Communicate Tile Positions
bullets: Call out tile positions, Key/Gate sightings, Wax Eater movements
Coordinate movement to avoid orphaning tiles
Announce your intended direction each turn

summary.title: Move Toward Keys Early
bullets: Explore in consistent directions, don't backtrack aimlessly
Every new tile is a chance to find a Key
Sooner everyone has Keys, sooner you can plan the escape

summary.title: When Lights Out Happens, Move Toward the Group
bullets: You MUST move during Lights Out — cannot Stay
Move TOWARD where you last saw other prisoners
Every step away from the group wastes tiles and makes rescue harder

summary.title: Wax Eaters: Avoid When Possible, Block When Not
bullets: First instinct: move away and let it chase you
When avoidance fails: spend 1 Nerve to Block (3 discards → 2)
Save Charging for when rerouting would cost more turns

summary.title: Don't Over-Explore — You're Draining the Tile Tower
bullets: Every move draws tiles from the tower, bringing Final Flickers closer
Explore toward objectives — Keys, Gates, other prisoners
Aimless wandering accelerates the endgame countdown

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Isolating from the group — bleed tiles, lose escape routes
Wasting Nerve on convenience — nothing left for real crises
Panic-moving during Lights Out — go toward the group, not away
Ignoring the tile tower — Final Flickers sneaks up on you

walkthrough.title: Tile Tower Management
walkthrough.text: The tile tower is the game's clock. Every tile drawn when you move brings you one step closer to Final Flickers. But tiles also go back to the bottom of the tower when darkness reclaims them, and when you Stay and discard an adjacent tile. Advanced players track the approximate tile count and adjust their urgency accordingly. When the tower is flush, explore freely and spread out to find Keys. When it is getting thin — maybe fifteen tiles left — tighten up your movement, stop wandering, converge on known Gate positions, and start banking Nerve for Sustain.

walkthrough.title: Optimal Movement Patterns for Key Hunting
walkthrough.text: Random wandering wastes tiles. The most efficient Key-hunting pattern is a sweeping arc from your Start Tile, covering new ground each turn without doubling back. Move in a consistent direction and let the darkness reclaim behind you — those tiles are going back to the bottom of the tower anyway, so future draws might bring Keys closer to the top. When two prisoners are exploring different arcs, they cover twice the ground. Coordinate directions so you are not exploring the same area. Once a Key is found, that prisoner should shift to convergence mode — heading toward the group rather than continuing to explore.

walkthrough.title: When to Stay vs When to Move
walkthrough.text: Staying costs one tile and one turn but gives you one Nerve. The calculus is straightforward. Stay when you have multiple adjacent tiles, no immediate threats, and your Nerve is below two. The ideal Stay position is a Crossroads tile — four connections give you maximum options afterward. Never Stay when you are adjacent to a Wax Eater — you are giving it a free turn to close the gap. Never Stay at two Nerve — the Nerve gain is wasted. And never Stay when time pressure demands forward progress — if the tower is thin and you have not found a Key, you need to move.

walkthrough.title: Charge vs Block Decision Tree
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater attacks, Block reduces the damage from three discards to two for one Nerve. When a Wax Eater is blocking the only path forward, Charge lets you move through it for one Nerve but you take the full three-discard hit. The decision tree: if the Wax Eater is NOT blocking a critical path, Block the attack and change direction. If the Wax Eater IS blocking the only route to a Key, Gate, or teammate, Charge through it. If you have two Nerve, consider Charging and then Blocking the next attack. Never Charge when you have an alternate route that costs only one or two extra turns — Charge's three-tile cost is brutal.

walkthrough.title: Sustain Timing During Final Flickers
walkthrough.text: Sustain is the most critical Nerve ability in the endgame. It prevents one tile from being removed after your turn during Final Flickers. The key restriction: you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn. This means you cannot Stay and Sustain on the same turn. The optimal strategy is to bank Nerve BEFORE Final Flickers begins. Start Staying to build Nerve when the tower drops below fifteen tiles. Two prisoners each with two Nerve can Sustain four times total — that is four extra turns of preserved maze. Coordinate who Sustains when — spread them across turns for maximum coverage.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Mode: Keeper Mechanics
walkthrough.text: In Advanced Mode, Keepers replace Keys entirely. Keepers have different pickup mechanics — instead of simply moving onto a Keeper tile and grabbing it, you may need to meet specific conditions. This fundamentally changes strategy because you cannot just run to a Keeper tile and grab it in one turn. Plan extra turns around each Keeper location. Communicate Keeper positions early so the team can coordinate who goes for which one. The rest of the escape rules remain the same — all prisoners still need their Keeper collected and must reach Gates — but the added complexity demands more Nerve reserves and tighter coordination.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Mode: Pit Fiend Avoidance Patterns
walkthrough.text: Pit Fiends are a second monster type added in Advanced Mode alongside Wax Eaters. They have different movement patterns — while Wax Eaters move toward the nearest prisoner along paths, Pit Fiends behave differently and create dead zones in the maze. The key to Pit Fiend avoidance is maintaining buffer tiles between you and the Fiend's patrol area. Never move directly toward a Pit Fiend — approach from an angle or reroute entirely. When both Wax Eaters and Pit Fiends are on the board, prioritize escaping Pit Fiend zones first, as their attacks are harder to mitigate with Nerve.

walkthrough.title: Coordinated Escape — Positioning for Gates
walkthrough.text: The endgame requires ALL prisoners to be holding Keys and standing on Gate tiles simultaneously. This means coordinated positioning is everything. Once three of four Keys are found, the team should shift from exploration mode to positioning mode. Identify where the known Gates are. Calculate how many turns each prisoner needs to reach one. Stagger your arrival — the first prisoner to reach a Gate should Stay on it and bank Nerve for Sustain while the others navigate. If a Gate is deep in the maze, the team should move together to keep the path illuminated. The worst scenario is a prisoner reaching a Gate but the path behind them going dark before everyone else arrives.

walkthrough.title: Sacrifice Plays
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move for the group is devastating for one prisoner. Moving away from the group to lure a Wax Eater away from a teammate carrying the last Key. Staying on a tile that will be consumed by darkness to buy another prisoner one more turn to reach a Gate. Charging through a monster so the path behind you is clear for a teammate. Advanced players recognize these moments and volunteer without hesitation. The question is never 'how do I survive?' — it is 'how do WE escape?' A sacrifice play that costs one prisoner their Nerve and puts them in Lights Out is worth it if it lets the rest of the team reach Gates.

walkthrough.title: Tower Depletion vs Tile Recycling
walkthrough.text: Advanced players understand the tower as a dynamic system, not just a countdown. Tiles leave the tower when you explore, but they return when darkness reclaims them and when you Stay. This means aggressive exploration does not permanently remove tiles — many cycle back. The real threat is net depletion: when you draw more tiles than the darkness returns. This happens when prisoners spread out and keep many tiles illuminated. Counterintuitively, sometimes letting tiles go dark is good — they recycle back into the tower, delaying Final Flickers. The optimal balance is keeping just enough tiles lit to maintain safe corridors while letting peripheral tiles recycle.

summary.title: Tile Tower Management
bullets: Track approximate tile count — the tower is the game's clock
Tower flush = explore freely; tower thin (~15 tiles) = tighten up
Tiles return to bottom when darkness reclaims them

summary.title: Optimal Movement Patterns for Key Hunting
bullets: Sweep arcs from Start Tile — cover new ground, don't double back
Coordinate directions so two prisoners don't explore the same area
Once a Key is found, shift to convergence mode toward the group

summary.title: When to Stay vs When to Move
bullets: Stay when safe, well-connected, and below 2 Nerve
Crossroads = ideal Stay position (4 connections)
Never Stay near Wax Eaters, at max Nerve, or under time pressure

summary.title: Charge vs Block Decision Tree
bullets: Block (1 Nerve): reduce attack from 3 discards to 2 — use when you can reroute
Charge (1 Nerve): move through monster, take full 3 discards — use when path is blocked
Never Charge when an alternate route costs only 1-2 extra turns

summary.title: Sustain Timing During Final Flickers
bullets: Cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn
Bank Nerve BEFORE Final Flickers begins (~15 tiles left)
2 prisoners × 2 Nerve = 4 Sustains — coordinate who Sustains when

bullets: Keepers replace Keys with different pickup conditions
Plan extra turns around each Keeper location
Communicate positions early — tighter coordination required

summary.title: Advanced Mode: Pit Fiend Avoidance Patterns
bullets: Pit Fiends have different movement than Wax Eaters — create dead zones
Maintain buffer tiles, approach from angles, don't move directly toward them
Prioritize escaping Pit Fiend zones over Wax Eater zones

summary.title: Coordinated Escape — Positioning for Gates
bullets: Shift from exploration to positioning once 3 of 4 Keys are found
First prisoner on a Gate should Stay and bank Nerve for Sustain
Move together to keep the path illuminated for everyone

summary.title: Sacrifice Plays
bullets: Lure Wax Eaters away, hold tiles, clear paths for teammates
Volunteer Lights Out or Nerve loss to protect Key carriers
The question is always 'how do WE escape?'

summary.title: Tower Depletion vs Tile Recycling
bullets: Tiles recycle: drawn when exploring, returned when darkness reclaims
Net depletion happens when too many tiles stay illuminated
Sometimes letting peripheral tiles go dark delays Final Flickers

entries.definition: A path tile connecting two opposite sides, forming a straight corridor. One of the three basic tile types. 10 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games.
A path tile connecting three sides, forming a T-shaped intersection. The most common tile type in the deck. 30 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games.
A path tile connecting all four sides, forming a full intersection. 12 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games. Crossroads are the most flexible tile — they connect in every direction.

A special tile containing a key. When a prisoner moves onto a Key tile, they pick up the key. Each prisoner needs one Key to escape through a Gate. 6 Keys for 4 players, 7 Keys for 5 players. Keys cannot be shared or traded between prisoners.
The escape tiles. Gates only activate when ALL prisoners are holding a Key. Once activated, each prisoner must move onto a Gate tile to complete the escape. 4 Gate tiles in all player counts.

Each prisoner begins the game on a Start Tile, placed face up and spread apart on the table. Start Tiles function as normal path tiles after the game begins. 4 Start Tiles for 4 players, 5 for 5 players.

When revealed from the tower, a Wax Eater miniature is placed on this tile. The creature then moves toward prisoners and attacks. 12 Wax Eaters for 4 players (4 in Advanced Mode), 10 for 5 players (2 in Advanced Mode).

At the end of each turn, each Wax Eater on the board moves one space along connected paths toward the nearest prisoner. Wax Eaters follow path connections — they cannot teleport or cross empty spaces.

When a Wax Eater reaches a prisoner's tile, it attacks. The attack forces the targeted prisoner to discard 3 tiles adjacent to their position. This can cascade into Lights Out if all surrounding tiles are consumed. Can be reduced to 2 discards with Block.

The primary action on your turn. Move your Prisoner meeple one space orthogonally (up, down, left, or right — not diagonal) to an adjacent tile. If moving to an empty space, draw and place a tile from the tower. After moving, reveal tiles for all empty adjacent spaces and remove unlit tiles.

The alternative to moving. Discard one tile adjacent to your position (your choice) to the bottom of the tower, then gain 1 Nerve (max 2). You cannot Stay if you are Lights Out. Staying costs a tile and a turn but banks emergency resources.
Spend 1 Nerve to take a second move this turn. Useful for closing distance to a Key, Gate, or teammate. Available in both Normal and Lights Out status.

Spend 1 Nerve to Stay even though you are in Lights Out status. Normally, Lights Out forces you to move. This overrides that restriction. You still discard a tile and gain 1 Nerve as normal (net zero Nerve since you spent 1 and gained 1). Gain no Nerve if already at max.

Spend 1 Nerve when a Wax Eater attacks you. Instead of discarding 3 adjacent tiles, you only discard 2. This is a reaction — used the instant an attack hits, not on your turn. The saved tile can prevent a cascade into Lights Out.

Spend 1 Nerve to move onto a Monster tile and immediately move through it to a connected tile on the other side. You take the full attack (3 tile discards) but end up past the monster. Desperate but effective when a Wax Eater blocks the only viable path.

Available only during Final Flickers. Spend 1 Nerve to prevent one illuminated tile from being removed after your turn. Critical restriction: you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn. This means you cannot Stay and Sustain on the same turn. Plan your Nerve banking before Final Flickers begins.

The emergency resource in The Night Cage. Gained by Staying (1 per Stay). Maximum of 2 Nerve per prisoner. Spent for special actions: Move Again, Stay (Lights Out), Block, Charge, and Sustain. Cannot exceed 2 regardless of source.

A status triggered when a prisoner has zero illuminated tiles adjacent to their position — they are surrounded by void. Flip the reference card to the Lights Out side. You MUST move (cannot Stay). Special Nerve options are available but restricted. Escape by moving to a space where new tiles can be drawn.

The endgame phase that begins when the tile tower is completely empty. No new tiles are drawn when moving to empty spaces. After each player's turn, one illuminated tile adjacent to them is removed (unless they Sustain). The maze rapidly collapses. Players must reach Gates before all tiles vanish.

Any tile that is adjacent to at least one prisoner. Illuminated tiles remain on the board. A tile that is not adjacent to any prisoner is considered dark and is removed to the bottom of the tower. Keeping tiles illuminated requires prisoners to stay near each other.

The vertical card holder where all shuffled tiles are stored face-down. Tiles are drawn from the top when prisoners explore. Removed tiles go to the bottom. When the tower empties, Final Flickers begins. The tower is the game's timer — every tile drawn brings the end closer.

Each player controls one Prisoner — a hooded figure trapped in the maze with only a candle. Prisoners are represented by colored wooden meeples (red, blue, green, yellow, purple) and matching tokens. All prisoners must escape together for the group to win.

Advanced mode tiles that replace Keys. Keepers have different pickup mechanics and add complexity to the escape objective. When using Keepers, remove all Key tiles from the deck and add Keeper tiles instead. Replaces Keys entirely. 6 Keepers for 4 players, 7 for 5 players.

Advanced mode monster tiles. Pit Fiends add a second type of creature to the maze alongside Wax Eaters. They have different movement and attack patterns. 2 Pit Fiend tiles are added to the deck in Advanced Mode.
Boss-level advanced tiles. Pathless tiles create areas of the maze with no path connections — dead zones that prisoners must navigate around. 2 Pathless tiles available in the full advanced deck.

Boss-level advanced tiles. Omens introduce special event-like effects when revealed from the tower. They add unpredictability and tension to the advanced game. 2 Omen tiles available in the full advanced deck.

Standard mode for 4 prisoners: 4 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 12 Wax Eaters. Advanced additions: 6 Keepers (replace Keys), 2 Pit Fiends, 2 Pathless, 2 Omens, 4 Wax Eaters (in Advanced Mode).

Standard mode for 5 prisoners: 5 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 10 Wax Eaters. Advanced additions: 7 Keepers (replace Keys), 2 Pit Fiends, 2 Pathless, 2 Omens, 2 Wax Eaters (in Advanced Mode).

Double-sided card given to each player. Normal side shows: YOUR TURN — Move One Space or Stay, plus all Nerve spending options. Lights Out side shows: YOUR TURN — Move One Space (you may not Stay), plus restricted Nerve spending options. Flip between sides as your status changes.
